source.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus Bronchiolitis in Children

signals: Treatment of RSV infection is mainly supportive
supplemental oxygen to maintain adequate oxygen saturation
intravenous or nasogastric administration of fluids
bronchodilators, epinephrine, corticosteroids...are generally not useful
clinical practice guideline for diagnosis and management

extraction.summary: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) bronchiolitis is a common lower respiratory tract infection in infants and young children presenting with upper respiratory symptoms followed by increased cough, wheezing, and respiratory effort. Treatment is primarily supportive care with oxygen supplementation and hydration maintenance, while avoiding unnecessary interventions like bronchodilators, corticosteroids, and antibiotics.

population.age_criteria: Primarily affects infants and children in first two years of life, with highest hospitalization rates in first 90 days of life
population.risk_criteria: Chronic lung disease, congenital anomalies, immunocompromised state, hemodynamically significant heart disease, premature birth, low birth weight (<2,300g)
population.disease_definition: Lower respiratory tract infection characterized by direct viral cytotoxic injury leading to necrosis of epithelial cells and bronchiolar plugging
population.severity_staging: Mild disease: adequate oral intake, age ≥2 months, initial O2 sat ≥94%, lower respiratory rate, no/mild retractions. Severe: younger age, dehydration, increased work of breathing, tachycardia (HR >97% for age)

key_thresholds.context: Minimum acceptable oxygen saturation for children with bronchiolitis
May indicate poor feeding and need for IV/NG hydration
Primary age group for RSV bronchiolitis

recommendations.recommendation: Routine viral testing and chest imaging are not recommended for patients with presumed RSV bronchiolitis
recommendations.notes: Does not affect management decisions or improve outcomes

recommendations.recommendation: Bronchodilators, systemic or inhaled corticosteroids, and epinephrine should not be administered to infants and children with bronchiolitis
recommendations.notes: No effect on hospitalization, oxygen saturation, or length of stay

recommendations.recommendation: Antibiotics should not be administered to children with RSV bronchiolitis unless bacterial infection is confirmed or suspected
recommendations.notes: Very low rate of bacteremia in bronchiolitis patients

recommendations.recommendation: Supportive care is the mainstay of treatment
recommendations.notes: Based on multiple systematic reviews and guidelines
Low risk of bacterial co-infection in typical cases
May reduce length of stay in hospitalized patients

medications.dose: Five monthly doses during RSV season
medications.indication: Prophylaxis for high-risk infants: <29 weeks gestation, chronic lung disease of prematurity, hemodynamically significant heart disease

medications.dose: As needed to maintain O2 sat ≥90%

medications.indication: Infants who cannot maintain oral hydration
May reduce hospital length of stay

red_flags: Increased work of breathing with retractions
Poor feeding or inability to maintain oral intake
Respiratory rate >60 breaths per minute in infants
Worsening respiratory status despite supportive care
extraction.follow_up: Monitor nutrition and hydration status, assess respiratory effort and oxygen saturation. Continuous pulse oximetry is optional. Educate parents on infection prevention including hand hygiene and avoiding crowds.
extraction.escalation_path: Hospitalize for: age <2 months, dehydration, increased work of breathing, tachycardia, inability to maintain oral intake, oxygen saturation <90%. Consider ICU for respiratory failure requiring intubation and mechanical ventilation (higher risk of bacterial pneumonia).
